items.title: AsyncRAT and Remcos delivered in an optimistic campaign
items.description:
A global phishing campaign targets business functions with emails carrying malicious Excel attachments that initiate a multi-stage infection chain when macros are enabled.
The attack uses layered obfuscation, including HTA scripts, PowerShell, encoded payloads, and steganography in PNG files, to deliver and execute Remote Access Trojans such as Remcos and AsyncRAT in a largely fileless manner.
It
items.ai_summary: The phishing campaign utilizes malicious Excel attachments to initiate a multi-stage infection chain, leveraging layered obfuscation techniques including HTA scripts, PowerShell, encoded payloads, and steganography in PNG files to deliver Remote Access Trojans such as Remcos and AsyncRAT. The attack vector involves T1566 phishing tactics, where emails are sent to business functions, and when macros are enabled, it triggers the infection chain. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, as it can lead to unauthorized access and control of infected systems, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems have the latest security patches, disable macros in Excel by default, and implement PowerShell restrictions, such as constraining language mode to prevent the execution of malicious scripts. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, including unusual network connections and process executions, and consider implementing a security orchestration, automation, and response (SOAR) solution to streamline incident response.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Impersonation, Click Hijacking, and TDS: Inside a Malware Distribution Ecosystem
items.description: A large-scale operation impersonates open-source and freeware projects to capture search traffic, targeting tools such as Ghidra, dnSpy, and SpiderFoot. The professionally designed sites load CloudFront-hosted JavaScript that converts download button clicks into handoffs to a Traffic Distribution System (TDS), which enforces strict gating including first-visit state, click confirmation, anti-bot l
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a large-scale operation that impersonates open-source and freeware projects, such as Ghidra, dnSpy, and SpiderFoot, to capture search traffic. The attackers use professionally designed sites that load CloudFront-hosted JavaScript, which converts download button clicks into handoffs to a Traffic Distribution System (TDS). This TDS enforces strict gating, including first-visit state and click confirmation, to filter out unwanted traffic. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust input validation and sanitization for all web applications, and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly those that are open-source or freeware. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on download buttons from unfamiliar sites and verify the authenticity of the site before proceeding.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: PCPJack Hijacked 230 AWS, GCP, and Azure Servers to Run a Hidden SMTP Relay Network
items.description: PCPJack operators compromised 230 cloud Linux servers across AWS, GCP, and Azure to build a covert SMTP relay network for email-based attacks. Researchers discovered exposed directories on infrastructure at 213.136.80[.]73 containing complete deployment toolkits including Chisel binaries, Python deployers, and operational state files. The campaign deployed Sliver C2 beacons and established reverse
items.ai_summary: The PCPJack threat actors have compromised 230 cloud Linux servers across AWS, GCP, and Azure to build a covert SMTP relay network for email-based attacks. The attackers exploited exposed directories on the infrastructure, deploying Chisel binaries, Python deployers, and operational state files to establish a hidden network. The campaign utilized Sliver C2 beacons to maintain remote access, leveraging T1072 software deployment tools and T1071 application layer protocols. To remediate, defenders should immediately review their cloud infrastructure for exposed directories and restrict access to only necessary personnel, updating their Linux servers to the latest versions and configuring firewalls to block suspicious traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor for T1219 remote access software and implement robust email security measures to prevent email-based attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1072| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1072| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Demon Arrives Later: A Havoc Stager Hides Behind Microsoft Defender DLP
items.description: Cybercriminals in Brazil are exploiting the country's electronic invoice system (Nota Fiscal eletrônica) to deliver Havoc framework implants. The campaign surfaced during May 2026, coinciding with tax season when accountants routinely process invoice-related emails. Attackers distribute malicious ZIP files disguised as legitimate invoices, containing VBScript droppers that download MSI installers
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a Havoc framework implant delivered through malicious ZIP files disguised as legitimate invoices, targeting Brazil's electronic invoice system. The attack vector involves VBScript droppers that download MSI installers, leveraging T1059.005 and masquerading as legitimate files using T1036. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and data breaches, particularly affecting accounting firms and businesses during tax season. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block ZIP files with VBScript attachments, update their antivirus software to detect Havoc framework implants, and educate employees on verifying the authenticity of invoice-related emails. Additionally, configuring Microsoft Defender to monitor for suspicious DLP activity can help detect similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.005| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Matryoshka #3/3: Gamaredon's Gammasteel Infostealer
items.description: This analysis examines Gamaredon's (UAC-0010, Armagedon) advanced espionage operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. The FSB-operated group deploys GammaSteel, a sophisticated stealer operating almost entirely from memory using Windows DPAPI encryption and storing 71 distinct payload functions in the HKCU\Printers registry key. The malware employs three con
items.ai_summary: Gamaredon's Gammasteel Infostealer is a sophisticated malware deployed by the FSB-operated group UAC-0010 (Armagedon) targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. The malware operates almost entirely from memory using Windows DPAPI encryption and stores 71 distinct payload functions in the HKCU\Printers registry key. If exploited, the malware can steal sensitive information, including login credentials and system data. To remediate, defenders should ensure Windows systems are updated to the latest version, specifically Windows 10 21H2 or later, and configure the registry to prevent unauthorized access to the HKCU\Printers key. Additionally, implement robust network segmentation and monitoring to detect and respond to potential infostealer activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Error 524 Decoy: Unmasking a Global Smishing Operation Hiding Behind Error Pages
items.description: A sophisticated smishing and phishing operation active since the second half of 2025 has impersonated over 267 brands across 72 countries, with particular concentration in Latin America. The campaign generated 4,389 phishing domain instances, with Mexico accounting for 1,851 cases. Telecommunications is the most targeted sector with 1,754 instances, followed by financial services and consumer rewa
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated smishing and phishing operation, active since the second half of 2025, impersonated over 267 brands across 72 countries, with a high concentration in Latin America. The campaign generated 4,389 phishing domain instances, with Mexico accounting for 1,851 cases. Telecommunications is the most targeted sector with 1,754 instances, followed by financial services and consumer rewards. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email authentication and verification processes, such as SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, and educate users on identifying and reporting suspicious emails. Additionally, organizations should monitor their email infrastructure for suspicious activity and implement a phishing simulation program to train employees.

items.title: Inside the Cross-Platform Propagation of a New Gafgyt Variant C0XMO
items.description: A new Gafgyt botnet variant named C0XMO has been discovered that spreads by exploiting a stack buffer overflow vulnerability in DD-WRT router firmware. Unlike earlier versions, this malware separates its lateral movement capabilities into a standalone Python script, enabling more efficient targeting of various system architectures including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, and x86. The malware establishes pers
items.ai_summary: A new variant of the Gafgyt botnet, named C0XMO, has been discovered exploiting a stack buffer overflow vulnerability in DD-WRT router firmware (no CVE ID provided). This vulnerability allows the malware to spread across various system architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, and x86, through a standalone Python script. If exploited, the malware can establish persistence and enable lateral movement, posing a significant threat to network security. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their DD-WRT firmware to the latest version (r44932) or higher, and ensure that the 'allow_exec' option is disabled in the 'Services' tab. Additionally, it is recommended to implement a network segmentation strategy to limit the spread of the malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1203| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.005| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Argamal: Malware hidden in hentai games
items.description: In April 2026, researchers discovered a malware campaign targeting players of adult-themed games. The infected games install a previously unknown implant called Argamal that downloads and executes a RAT after several days, resulting in full system compromise. The malware uses COM hijacking to persist, replacing the InprocServer32 entry for Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL. Delivery occu
items.ai_summary: The Argamal malware campaign targets players of adult-themed games, installing a previously unknown implant that downloads and executes a Remote Access Tool (RAT) after several days, resulting in full system compromise. The malware uses COM hijacking (T1546.015) to persist, replacing the InprocServer32 entry for the Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL. This allows the malware to maintain persistence and execute arbitrary code on the compromised system. To remediate, users should update to Windows 10 version 21H2 or later, which includes a fix for the COM hijacking vulnerability. Additionally, users should ensure that their systems are running the latest version of the Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL, which can be downloaded from the official Microsoft website.

graph LR

    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Deno Abuse to CastleRAT
items.description: Activity began with a ClickFix-style social engineering chain that led to MSI execution, PowerShell staging, and installation/use of Deno to run attacker-controlled JavaScript. Follow-on activity downloaded a portable Python runtime, `install.pyc`, and an encrypted `.MOa` container, which was later decrypted to recover a 64-bit Windows PE payload. Analysis of the recovered payload showed Steam Com
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix Deno Abuse to CastleRAT threat involves a sophisticated social engineering chain that leverages MSI execution and PowerShell staging to install Deno, a JavaScript runtime, which is then used to run attacker-controlled JavaScript. This allows the threat actor to download and execute a 64-bit Windows PE payload, which is recovered from an encrypted container. The threat actor uses T1059.001 (PowerShell) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to achieve this. To remediate, defenders should ensure Deno is not installed on vulnerable systems and remove any existing instances. Additionally, users should be educated on the risks of executing MSI files and PowerShell scripts from untrusted sources. Furthermore, systems should be updated to the latest version of Deno to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:

    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest goon squad to use fake helpdesk calls to steal creds
items.description: A new extortion group called Pink, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, employs voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations. The gang steals employee credentials, bypasses multi-factor authentication, and exfiltrates data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive. Pink threatens to leak stolen information unless ransom demands are met, setting 72-hour d
items.ai_summary: The Pink extortion group, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, employs voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations. The attackers use T1598.004 (Spearphishing Voice) to trick employees into divulging credentials, which they then bypass multi-factor authentication to access. The group exfiltrates data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive using T1213 (Data from Information Repositories) and T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel). To prevent this, organizations should implement robust voice phishing detection and prevention measures, such as voice biometrics and AI-powered call analysis. Additionally, employees should be educated on the dangers of fake IT helpdesk calls and the importance of verifying caller identities.

    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-54037: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prio...
items.description: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prior to 0.8.4-rc1, the fix for CVE-2025-7105 added forkIpLimiter and forkUserLimiter rate limiters to POST /api/convos/fork to prevent rapid-fire conversation duplication. However, the POST /api/convos/duplicate endpoint — which is in the same file and performs the exact same expensive database operations — was not given
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54037 is a vulnerability in LibreChat, a ChatGPT clone, prior to version 0.8.4-rc1. The issue arises from the lack of rate limiters on the POST /api/convos/duplicate endpoint, which performs expensive database operations. This allows an attacker to conduct a denial-of-service (DoS) attack by repeatedly calling the endpoint, leading to resource exhaustion and potentially causing the application to become unresponsive. To remediate this issue, users should update to version 0.8.4-rc1 or later, which includes the necessary rate limiters. Additionally, administrators should monitor their application's performance and adjust the rate limiters as needed to prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploitation of Vulnerability]:::tactic
    B -->|T1210| C[Denial of Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-54033: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prio...
items.description: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prior to 0.8.4-rc1, LibreChat allows users to configure custom OpenAI-compatible API endpoints by setting a baseURL. This URL is used to construct HTTP requests without any SSRF validation — no private IP check, no scheme restriction, no DNS pinning. An authenticated user can set baseURL to internal network addresses. This
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54033 is a SSRF vulnerability in LibreChat, a ChatGPT clone, prior to version 0.8.4-rc1. An authenticated user can set a custom OpenAI-compatible API endpoint by configuring the baseURL, which is used to construct HTTP requests without validation. This allows an attacker to access internal network addresses, potentially leading to data exfiltration or lateral movement. To remediate, update to version 0.8.4-rc1 or later, and ensure that the baseURL is set to a trusted, publicly accessible URL. Additionally, consider implementing DNS pinning and private IP checks to further mitigate the risk. LibreChat users who have not updated to the latest version are at risk of exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-54030: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prio...
items.description: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prior to 0.8.5, LibreChat's MCP OAuth implementation does not validate that the resource parameter from OAuth Protected Resource metadata (RFC 9728) matches the configured MCP server URL, allowing a malicious MCP server to steal access tokens intended for a legitimate server. This vulnerability is fixed in 0.8.5.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54030 is a critical vulnerability in LibreChat, a ChatGPT clone, prior to version 0.8.5. The issue lies in the MCP OAuth implementation, which fails to validate the resource parameter from OAuth Protected Resource metadata, allowing a malicious MCP server to steal access tokens intended for a legitimate server. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker to gain unauthorized access to user accounts, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this issue, users should update to version 0.8.5 or later, and ensure that the MCP server URL is properly configured. Additionally, administrators should monitor their systems for any suspicious activity related to OAuth token theft.

items.title: CVE-2026-54029: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prio...
items.description: LibreChat is an enhanced ChatGPT clone that supports multiple AI providers. Prior to 0.8.4-rc1, the DELETE /api/messages/:conversationId/:messageId endpoint allows any authenticated user to delete any other user's messages. The validateMessageReq middleware only validates that the conversationId belongs to the requesting user, but the handler calls deleteMessages({ messageId }) using only the mess
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54029 is a vulnerability in LibreChat, a ChatGPT clone, prior to version 0.8.4-rc1. The DELETE /api/messages/:conversationId/:messageId endpoint allows any authenticated user to delete any other user's messages due to insufficient validation. This allows an attacker to delete messages from other users, potentially disrupting communication and compromising sensitive information. To remediate, update to version 0.8.4-rc1 or later, and ensure the validateMessageReq middleware is properly configured to validate both conversationId and messageId. Additionally, consider implementing additional access controls to restrict message deletion to authorized users.

A permission check was added to the POST /api/files route in a previous patch, but the image
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-54027 is a vulnerability in LibreChat, a ChatGPT clone, prior to version 0.8.4-rc1. The vulnerability allows any authenticated user to upload files into any agent's tool_resources without verifying ownership or EDIT permission on the target agent. This can lead to unauthorized code execution or data exfiltration. To remediate, update to version 0.8.4-rc1 or later, and ensure that the POST /api/files/images endpoint is properly configured to enforce ownership and EDIT permission checks. Additionally, ensure that all users are properly authenticated and authorized before allowing file uploads.

When such a packet is handled by the fragment-header processing path, the associated RX network packet buffer (allocated from a memory slab) is not released back to the pool. items.title: Unpatched Flaws Disclosed in Filesystem Bundled Into Millions of Embedded Devices
items.description: Security firm runZero has disclosed seven vulnerabilities in FatFs, a small filesystem library that lets a device read and write the FAT and exFAT formats used on USB drives and SD cards. items.title: New "Bad Epoll" Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Unprivileged Users Gain Root, Hits Android
items.description: A newly disclosed Linux kernel flaw called Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242) lets an ordinary user with no special access take full control of a machine as root. It affects Linux desktops, servers, and Android, and a fix is out. Avalon combines credential collection, lateral movement, remote access, recovery disruption, and ransomware execution, bringing together diverse functions under one

items.title: NetNut proxy network disrupted, 2 million infected devices cut off
items.description: A joint operation involving Google has disrupted NetNut, a residential proxy network that gave access to millions of compromised Android devices, including smart TVs and streaming boxes. [...]

items.title: North Korea-Linked npm Packages Mimic Rollup Polyfills to Steal Developer Secrets
items.description: Threat actors with ties to North Korea have been linked to a fresh set of malicious npm packages that masquerade as Rollup polyfill tooling to facilitate remote access and data theft. According to JFrog, the packages "rollup-packages-polyfill-core" and "rollup-runtime-polyfill-core" mimic the legitimate "rollup-plugin-polyfill-node" project, down to the description, repository metadata, and

items.title: In Other News: Canadian Hacker Jailed, Open Source Zero-Days, Two Sentenced for ATM Jackpotting
items.description: Noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Anonymous-linked Canadian hacker jailed, researcher drops zero-days in open source projects, Venezuelans sentenced in the US over ATM jackpotting. The post In Other News: Canadian Hacker Jailed, Open Source Zero-Days, Two Sentenced for ATM Jackpotting appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ARToken PhaaS exposes EvilTokens' Microsoft 365 phishing toolkit
items.description: A new phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform dubbed "ARToken" appears to operate as an affiliate of the EvilTokens phishing platform, giving researchers a glimpse into an extensive toolkit designed to compromise Microsoft 365. [...]

items.title: Armored Likho Targets Government Agencies, Power Sector with BusySnake Stealer
items.description: A previously undocumented threat actor known as Armored Likho has been attributed to cyber attacks targeting government agencies and the electric power sector across Russia, Brazil, and Kazakhstan. "Armored Likho blends financially motivated campaigns targeting private individuals with targeted cyber espionage aimed at organizations," Kaspersky said in a technical analysis published today. "

items.title: Chinese LLMs Broaden the Gap Between Attackers & Defenders
items.description: Two new models from Chinese firms compete with top US mainstream and frontier models. Should cyber-defenders be worried?

items.title: European Parliament Member Investigating Spyware Was Hacked With Pegasus
items.description: A new report from the Citizen Lab has revealed that former Member of the European Parliament Stelios Kouloglou had his mobile device repeatedly hacked with the notorious Pegasus spyware while serving on a committee that was tasked with investigating the abuse of such commercial surveillance tools in the bloc. "Through forensic analysis of his device, we found that the attackers could have had

items.title: Agentic AI Used to Conduct Ransomware Attack via Langflow
items.description: Attack demonstrates how LLM agents can combine known exploitation techniques with real-time reasoning to automate complex, multi-stage intrusions. The post Agentic AI Used to Conduct Ransomware Attack via Langflow appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Medtronic Data Breach Impacts 3.8 Million People
items.description: In April, ShinyHunters accessed the company’s corporate IT systems and stole patients’ personal and medical information. The post Medtronic Data Breach Impacts 3.8 Million People appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Alleged Scattered Spider Hacker Extradited to US
items.description: Prosecutors say 19-year-old Peter Stokes was a member of Scattered Spider, the hacking group linked to more than 100 network intrusions and over $100 million in ransom payments. The post Alleged Scattered Spider Hacker Extradited to US appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google, FBI Disrupt NetNut Residential Proxy Network Powered by Millions of Devices
items.description: NetNut rented access to millions of compromised devices, allowing cybercriminals and nation-state actors to mask their identities during attacks. The post Google, FBI Disrupt NetNut Residential Proxy Network Powered by Millions of Devices appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: PamStealer Uses Fake Maccy Sites and PAM Checks to Steal Mac Login Passwords
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new macOS information stealer called PamStealer that employs a series of clever tricks to infect systems and siphon sensitive data. The stealer, discovered by Jamf Threat Labs, is distributed as a compiled AppleScript (.scpt) file impersonating Maccy, a legitimate open-source clipboard manager. It has been codenamed PamStealer owing to its ability to

items.title: Critical Cursor AI Code Editor Flaws Could Lead to OS-Level Remote Code Execution
items.description: The DuneSlide vulnerabilities enable zero-click prompt injection attacks that escape Cursor's sandbox and execute arbitrary code on the underlying operating system. The post Critical Cursor AI Code Editor Flaws Could Lead to OS-Level Remote Code Execution appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Spyware found on phone of European Parliament member probing it
items.description: Stelios Kouloglou, formerly a member of the European Parliament's committee investigating abuses of commercial spyware, was twice infected with Pegasus while serving, researchers said.

items.title: Claude Fable 5 isn’t permanently leaving subscriptions, Anthropic says
items.description: Anthropic says Claude Fable 5 won't be accessible via Claude subscriptions after July 7, but it's not a permanent change, and the company expects the model to return outside the usage-based plan soon. [...]

items.title: Claude Fable relaunch disappoints users with nerfed performance
items.description: Claude Fable, the company's most powerful model, is now available to all users, but early impressions are disappointing, as it appears to be nowhere near the original release. [...]

items.title: Aussies Face Reduced Cybercrime Risk, as Pressure Shifts to SMBs
items.description: Improved institutional safeguards and stricter regulations have pushed the burdens of protection and risk reduction on to Australian businesses.

items.title: Launch of UK's National Cyber Action Plan delayed amid Labour leadership crisis
items.description: The plan had been due for publication on Monday, the sources said. It has been postponed amid the uncertainty over the governing Labour Party’s leadership contest, which opens July 9.

items.title: Apple Reverses Age-Old Patch Policy to Keep Up With AI
items.description: Expect more compressed patching cycles from Apple going forward, as attackers leverage artificial intelligence to reduce time to exploit.

items.title: FBI Seizes NetNut Proxy Platform, Popa Botnet
items.description: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) said today it worked with industry partners to seize hundreds of domains associated with NetNut, a sprawling residential proxy service operated by the publicly-traded Israeli company Alarum Technologies [NASDAQ: ALAR]. The action comes roughly two weeks after KrebsOnSecurity published findings from multiple security firms connecting NetNut to the Popa botnet, a collection of at least two million devices that have been compromised by malicious software with little or no consent from victims.

items.title: FortiBleed Actors Collaborating With Inc, Lynx Ransomware Gangs
items.description: After gaining a foothold in thousands of Fortinet firewalls, the attackers are starting to monetize that access, and are also piling on a Nextcloud zero-day bug.

items.title: Google Disrupts NetNut Residential Proxy Network Spanning 2 Million Home Devices
items.description: Google has significantly degraded NetNut, one of the biggest networks that turns home devices into rented relays for other people's traffic. Working with the FBI, Lumen, and others, Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) said this week it had reduced the network's pool of usable devices by millions. Google identifies NetNut, also tracked as Popa, as a network spread across home

items.title: Ransomware Thugs Masquerade as Interpol to Entice Small Biz
items.description: The ransomware campaign relies on basic social engineering and stretches across multiple regions, including the US, Europe, Middle East, and elsewhere.

items.title: Supreme Court decision threatens EU-US data transfer agreement
items.description: In a Tuesday letter, Max Schrems, the founder of the Vienna-based privacy advocacy organization noyb, told European officials he plans to sue to invalidate the EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework (DPF) that allows for the transfer of personal data from the EU to U.S. companies.

items.title: Google loses final appeal to overturn €4.1 billion EU fine
items.description: Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) has dismissed Google's final appeal against a €4.1 billion ($4.7 billion) antitrust fine over the company's use of Android to promote its Chrome browser and search service. [...]

items.title: New CitrixBleed Vulnerability Exploited Immediately After Public Disclosure
items.description: Hackers are targeting NetScaler appliances using public PoC code to retrieve arbitrary memory content in the HTTP response. The post New CitrixBleed Vulnerability Exploited Immediately After Public Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ConsentFix and ClickFix: How Microsoft 365 Accounts are Hijacked in 3 Seconds
items.description: ConsentFix and ClickFix attacks steal Microsoft 365 tokens in seconds using fake prompts and OAuth flows. Learn how these MFA bypass tactics work and how to defend against them. [...]

items.title: How to Conduct a Successful Audit of AI-Driven Software Development
items.description: As AI-generated code becomes commonplace, CISOs need new audit strategies to measure developer practices, govern AI tool usage, and identify software risks before they reach production. The post How to Conduct a Successful Audit of AI-Driven Software Development appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FortiBleed Campaign Linked to INC, Lynx Ransomware Attacks
items.description: Researchers say credentials harvested from hundreds of thousands of FortiGate firewalls are being used to facilitate ransomware attacks by the INC and Lynx operations. The post FortiBleed Campaign Linked to INC, Lynx Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic's AI Finds Bugs. IBM Bets $5B It Can Fix Them.
items.description: IBM and Red Hat assign 20,000 engineers to the new Project Lightwell service as Anthropic's Mythos findings ignite debate over how to secure the open-source software supply chain.

items.title: Microsoft fixes bug that removed Copilot buttons in Outlook
items.description: Microsoft has fixed a known issue causing the Copilot Chat or Copilot buttons in Classic Outlook to disappear for Windows users with the Copilot Chat (Basic) license. [...]

items.title: Cisco finally confirms attackers exploiting Unified CM flaw
items.description: Cisco confirmed that attackers are now exploiting a Unified Communications Manager (Unified CM) vulnerability patched in early June. [...]

items.title: Trump Administration Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Claude Models After Cybersecurity Alarm
items.description: Anthropic said Tuesday night that its AI model called Claude Fable 5 is now widely available. The post Trump Administration Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Claude Models After Cybersecurity Alarm appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, July 2nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9992, (Thu, Jul 2nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Crafty Phishing Campaigns Auto-Adapt to Victim's Device, OS
items.description: Attackers fingerprint victims through user-agent data to deliver OS-specific payloads, increasing compromise rates and campaign profitability.

items.title: Teen suspect in Scattered Spider hacks is extradited to US
items.description: A complaint unsealed this week accuses a 19-year-old of participating in incidents including a breach of a "luxury-jewelry retailer" in 2025.

items.title: And the Winner in Dominant Malware Delivery? ClickFix
items.description: Researchers say the highly effective social engineering technique is no longer the exception for malware attacks — it's now the rule.

items.title: When Too Much Security Data Became the Risk
items.description: Rapid growth turned routine firewall logs into a security and budget liability. One CISO used artificial intelligence to filter what data truly belongs in the SIEM.

items.title: 'Phantom Squatting': An Emerging AI-Driven Supply Chain Threat
items.description: LLMs consistently hallucinate Web domains for legitimate brands that attackers can register for malicious activity in a difficult-to-detect attack vector.

items.title: US lifts export controls on Anthropic’s frontier cybersecurity AI models
items.description: Anthropic said export controls on certain models had been lifted after the company came to a series of agreements with the government.

items.title: Why Ask Credentials If There Are Secret Codes?, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: This morning, an interesting phishing email hit my mailbox. It targets Metamask[1], a cryptocurrency wallet, available as a browser extension and a mobile app, that lets users store, send, and receive crypto money. It&#x27s pretty popular, so a juicy target for criminals. In February, I already mentioned a campaign against them[2].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, July 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9990, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: June 2026 Apple Updates, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: Apple released updates for iOS/iPadOS, macOS, and Safari on Monday. There have been no updates for other Apple operating systems (visionOS, watchOS, tvOS). Usually, Apple updates all products at the same time.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 30th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9988, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Adding some Automation to the favicon.ico method of Host Recon, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;m in the throes of target host recon for another pentest, and thought I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;d share some workflow / automation stuff. In the past, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve discussed using historic DNS "mining" to collect target hosts in the domain.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9986, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.18.0 and 1.19.0 Release, (Sun, Jun 28th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.18.0 release brings 3 improvements and 2 bugfixes.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
